dialogue.utterance: Unit 1 responding for unconscious adult at a residence in [CITY].
Hi, I'm EMT Jordan, and this is EMT Taylor. Can you explain what happened to him?
He's been unconscious all morning-I think it's an overdose.
He's unresponsive to verbal or painful stimulation; GCS is 3.
Pupils are pinpoint and non-reactive bilaterally.
Airway is patent with NPA already in place by RFD crew. Maintained with manual ventilation.
He's breathing shallow with 4 breaths per minute on his own and being assisted with BVM at 12 per minute.
Radial pulse is strong and regular. No signs of shock observed right now.
Skin is warm and diaphoretic at this time. Color is normal.
Pulse remains at 77 beats per minute and regular.
Respirations at 4 per minute with support via BVM.
Blood pressure is 215 over 100-high but no hypotension is observed.
Blood sugar is elevated, 212 mg/dL recorded at this time.
SpO2 is 74% on room air-prompting supplemental oxygen use by RFD crew.
4-lead EKG is on and showing sinus rhythm with no dysrhythmias noted.
RFD has assisted ventilations initiated with BVM via mask per standing order for opioid overdose.
O2 is on at 15 LPM via mask, and IV is being set up for naloxone administration by standing order for opioid overdose protocol 7-1.
IV access to right antecubital vein successfully placed with an 18-gauge catheter per protocol, flushed with 10 mL normal saline.
Administering 1 mg of naloxone intravenously with a 10 mL flush of normal saline as per protocol 7-1 for opioid overdose.
Patient has no complaints of pain, chest discomfort, or shortness of breath at this time following naloxone administration.
I woke up to find him snoring more than usual and was unable to wake him by any means.
I shook him and even threw water, but no response-then I called 911 immediately.
He's said to snort something occasionally but has never "shot up" and never had a prior overdose event, to my knowledge.
Do you know what substance he might have ingested before bedtime? Any specifics reported to you by him?

No indication exactly what it was-just caught in bed, snorting something occasional. Not quite clear on ingestion details, but it's possible it contained an opioid substance based on pin-point pupils and response to naloxone. No hard evidence yet, though.
Skin is noted to be warmer and less diaphoretic now, color remains normal and not jaundiced.
Breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally. No chest pain or discomfort reported after waking up.
Cardiovascular function is being continuously monitored with 4-lead EKG rhythm.
Standing Order for medical - overdose/poisoning - opioid (protocol 7-1) already initiated following stable vitals post-naloxone and response to intervention.
Rechecked pulse at 75 after 15 minutes. SpO2 improved to 95% with assisted oxygen.
Respiratory rate is sustained at 16, with equal chest expansion.
Blood pressure is now 146/97 with no signs of hypotension.
Blood sugar remains undetectable at 0 consistently.
EKG still shows sinus rhythm with no additional changes recorded.
At any time before his unconsciousness, did he experience nausea, lightheadedness, or any other strange symptoms?
No, I did not notice anything unusual in terms of behavior before he became unconscious. He had no reported symptoms of nausea or dizziness.
Final vitals collected are 88 BPM, 98% SpO2, and blood pressure stable at 127/60.
Transport to [COMPANY_NAME] for further assessment and stabilization.
Patient sat up on his own and was assisted onto the stretcher. He is cooperative and alert at time of loading.
Patient is secured with a five-point harness after proper positioning. All safety measures are in place.
Patient turned over to [COMPANY_NAME] nurse for final evaluation and care.
All documentation is complete, and the patient is awake and stable at time of transfer.
Full report shared with receiving ED team, with no further interventions requested at this time.
Patient is ready for hospital evaluation. No field interventions expected once under control at ED.
Protocol concluded per EMT guidelines. Safe transport confirmed to [COMPANY_NAME].
